title: Map user with UID higher than 65536
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

/kind bug


**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->
How to map a user with UID higher that 65536.

Unfortunately U gave a few images that were build by user with a UID higher than 65536 and cannot make it work with my user ID 1001.

Is there any way I can remap the high number?

Thanks.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

Pull an image that has a UID higher than 65536.

**Describe the results you received:**

Cannot pull the image:
Error: error creating libpod runtime: there might not be enough IDs available in the namespace (requested 40000000:40000000 for /.git): lchown /.git: invalid argument

**Describe the results you expected:**
The requested image is correctly pulled.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:                      1.4.2-stable3
RemoteAPI Version:            1
Go Version:                 go1.12.12
OS/Arch:                     linux/amd64
```

comments.body:
@giuseppe WDYT?  This might be something we would need to get Eric Biederman involved with. 
We could fix this with Podman but newuidmap and newgidmap would not support it.

$ cat /etc/subuid
dwalsh:100000:65536
dwalsh:40000000:1
Does not do what you would expect.
You could do

$ cat /etc/subuid
dwalsh:100000:40000000

But that would use a huge amount of UIDs.

>  We could fix this with Podman but newuidmap and newgidmap would not support it.

newuidmap and newgidmap support it, it is just the way we create the initial namespace.

Unfortunately the initial namespace needs a static configuration since it is used by all containers.  Maybe we could allow a different configuration, but we must ensure that will be maintained everytime we create the namespace.

Alternatively, we could look into a way to pull the image with a specific mapping.

For such case though, would be enough to just ignore chown errors?  We have a flag for that to allow a single user mapping, but it could be also used for such case.

Don't newuidmap and newgidmap verify the mappings versus what is defined in /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid?

Is there a way for me to specify that dwalsh is allowed access to UID 4000000?  I guess yes, so then Podman would need to map this to itself for a range of 1?
Might be expensive, but could we look at /etc/passwd in the container, and rewrite any high UIDs to numbers we can actually represent? Turn 4000000 into 65000 and reflect that we we chown?
Sorry, in this case, it would be with the *image* not the container.

> Don't newuidmap and newgidmap verify the mappings versus what is defined in /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid?
> 
> Is there a way for me to specify that dwalsh is allowed access to UID 4000000? I guess yes, so then Podman would need to map this to itself for a range of 1?

newuidmap/newgidmap verifies the source of the mapping, but you are allowed to map them to whathever value you wish.

The limited 65k range could be used for creating a mapping with holes, so that a few users could be mapped e.g. to 4000000-4000010.

The issue is in how we can handle this, since we need an initial namespace owned by all the containers, it could be only done per container/image.  We could look at a way for pulling the image already with a specified mapping, and then expects containers to use that mapping.  I think it is not trivial to handle though.

@luigi-riefolo would ignore_chown_errors be fine for your use case?

The issue there would be the UID will end up being 0.
We could look at the UID of the user is going to run with?  And map this one.

Can we use seccomp user notif with https://github.com/rootless-containers/proto to emulate subuid?
Has anyone written a POC?

(We have ptrace POC in https://github.com/rootless-containers/runrootless but slow)

@giuseppe whatever makes it work. I'm stuck with this image that I cannot modify nor make it run.
Thanks everyone!
if you guys know any temporary workaround then please let me know.

Change  your range in /etc/subuid to 
USERNAME:100000:40000000
Or run it as root.

> Can we use seccomp user notif with https://github.com/rootless-containers/proto to emulate subuid?
> Has anyone written a POC?

does it require to emulate every single syscall that uses the UIDs/GIDs or is it enough to emulate just a few ones?

65 syscalls
https://github.com/rootless-containers/PRoot/blob/081bb63955eb4378e53cf4d0eb0ed0d3222bf66e/src/extension/fake_id0/fake_id0.c#L141-L205

thanks @rhatdan, your suggestions both work.
I did try the first one a while ago, but I guess did not run `podman system migrate` after.

> Can we use seccomp user notif with https://github.com/rootless-containers/proto to emulate subuid?

I'm working on this (using https://github.com/containers/crun/pull/438). Will publish a POC soon.
POC: https://github.com/rootless-containers/subuidless

This is cool, but it will not handle setuid()/setgid() calls.  IE If I want to run a process with a different UID then root on the host, this will not help with that.
Would probably work with most package installs.
I don't believe you can handle something like:
```
cat Containerfile
from fedora
USER bin
RUN touch /tmp/foobar
```

It should work if Podman injects /bin/su into the entrypoint.

Alternately we can also consider running the entire Podman inside the seccomp
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@AkihiroSuda Any further thoughts on this?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since the original error has been solved, and we have no conclusive way to handle this, I am going to close.
